elements.sent-2411-001: In this work, we propose a new way to (non-interactively, verifiably) demonstrate Quantum Advantage by solving the average-case $\mathsf{NP}$ search problem of finding a solution to a system of (underdetermined) multivariate quadratic equations over the finite field $\mathbb{F _2$ drawn from a specified distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we design a distribution of degree-2 polynomials $\{p_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\}_{i\in [m]}$ for $m<n$ over $\mathbb{F _2$ for which we show that there is a quantum polynomial-time algorithm that simultaneously solves $\{p_i(x_1,\ldots,x_n)=y_i\}_{i\in [m]}$ for a random vector $(y_1,\ldots,y_m)$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: On the other hand, while a solution exists with high probability, we conjecture that it is classically hard to find one based on classical cryptanalysis that we provide, including a comprehensive review of all known relevant classical algorithms for solving multivariate quadratics.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our approach proceeds by examining the Yamakawa-Zhandry (FOCS 2022) quantum advantage scheme and replacing the role of the random oracle with our multivariate quadratic equations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our work therefore gives several new perspectives:.
elements.sent-2411-006: Cryptography based on multivariate quadratic equations over a finite field has a history that spans over four decades.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recent interest in this area has been spurred by the potential for using such systems for post-quantum security.
elements.sent-2411-008: Up until now, no super-polynomial quantum speedup for solving any system of quadratic equations over any finite field has been known, to the best of our knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our algorithm therefore gives a counterexample to the conventional belief that generic classically hard multivariate quadratic systems are also quantumly hard.
elements.sent-2411-010: While the quadratic systems typically encountered in current multivariate schemes are in a different parameter regime from our polynomial system, we believe that our technique might be useful towards discovering many other distributions of polynomials that are quantumly easy.
elements.sent-2411-011: Characterizing these families is an immediately important goal, as some could be relevant to the cryptanalysis of multivariate schemes and more broadly to any cryptographic schemes vulnerable to polynomial system solving attacks.
elements.sent-2411-012: Second, based on cryptanalytic evidence, our work gives an explicit simple replacement for the random oracle from the work of Yamakawa and Zhandry.
elements.sent-2411-013: We show how to instantiate the random oracle with families of just degree two multivariate polynomials over $\mathbb{F _2$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: We observe that this degree is tight in the sense that replacing the random oracle with degree one functions would be trivially easy to solve classically.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our work thus posits that degree two functions are enough to instantiate the random oracle to obtain non-relativized quantum advantage.
elements.sent-2411-016: On the technical side, our proof of quantum advantage significantly deviates from Yamakawa-Zhandry in that instead of relying on properties of a random oracle, we must leverage the structure of the variety of solutions to random quadratics over $\mathbb{F _2$ to execute our proof strategy.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \label{algo:1} The algorithm is parameterized by a constant decoding parameter $\varepsilon > 0$. The inputs to the algorithm are the description of system of degree two polynomials $\cP = \{p_i \in \bF_2[x_{i, 1}, \ldots, x_{i, n}]\}$ for the indeterminates $(x_{1, 1}, \ldots, x_{1, n}, \ldots, x_{m, 1}, \ldots, x_{m, n})$ sampled as specified in Section~\ref{sec:mqsystem} and the generating matrix for the dual code $\mbf{H} \in \bF_{2^{n}}^{(1-\alpha)m \times m}$. \label{subsec:alg} \begin{enumerate} \item \label{step:1} For each $i \in [m]$, prepare the uniform superposition of preimages over $\bF_2^{n}$ of $0$ under $p_i$, \[\ket{\phi_i} \propto \sum_{\mbf{e}_i \in \bF_2^{n} : p_i(\mbf{e}_i) = 0} \ket{\mbf{e}_i}\] Define the state \[\ket{\Phi} \coloneqq \bigotimes_{i =1}^{m} \ket{\phi_i} \propto \sum_{\mbf{e} = (\mbf{e}_1, \ldots, \mbf{e}_m) \in \bF_2^{mn}: \forall i \in [m] p_i(\mbf{e}_i) = 0}\ket{\mbf{e}}.\] \item Prepare a uniform superposition over all codewords\footnote{Both primal and dual code is over $\bF_{2^n}$. See Remark~\ref{rem:extinterpret}.} for the primal code $\primal$: \[\ket{\Psi} \propto \sum_{\mbf{x} \in \primal} \ket{\mbf{x}},\] In more detail, starting from the uniform superposition $2^{-\alpha m/2} \cdot \sum_{\Tilde{\mbf{x}} \in \bF_{2^n}^{\alpha m}} \ket{\Tilde{\mbf{x}}}$, compute the generating matrix $\mbf{G}$ of the primal code $\primal$ from $\mbf{H}$ and then compute $\ket{\mbf{G} \cdot \Tilde{\mbf{x}}}$ from $\ket{\Tilde{\mbf{x}}}$ to obtain a uniform superposition over all codewords of the primal code, $\ket{\Psi} \propto \sum_{\mbf{x} \in \primal} \ket{\mbf{x}}$. \item Compute $\ket{\hat{\Phi}} = \QFT_{2^n} \ket{\Phi}$. The QFT is taken over the extension field $\bF_{2^n}$. Compute the QFT of $\ket{\hat{\Psi}} = \QFT_{2^n} \ket{\Psi}$ over $\bF_{2^n}$. \item Perform the unitary operation $U_{\mathsf{add}} \left( \ket{\hat{\Psi}} \otimes \ket{\hat{\Phi}} \right)$ which adds the first register to the second register, i.e., \[ \ket{\mbf{x}} \ket{\mbf{e}} \xrightarrow{U_{\mathsf{add}}} \ket{\mbf{x}} \ket{\mbf{e} + \mbf{x}}\] \item Perform the unitary operation $U_{\mathsf{decode}}$ on the quantum state $ U_{\mathsf{add}} \left( \ket{\hat{\Psi}} \otimes \ket{\hat{\Phi}} \right)$ that first applies the decoding algorithm $\decode$ for $\dual$ to the second register and then subtracts the result from the first register, i.e., \[ \ket{\mbf{x}} \ket{\mbf{e} + \mbf{x}} \xrightarrow{U_{\mathsf{decode}}}\ket{\mbf{x}-\decode(\mat{e}+\mat{x})} \ket{\mbf{e} + \mbf{x}}.\] For a constant $0< \epsilon < \frac{1}{2} $, $\decode$ is given by: \begin{mdframed} $\decode(\mat{z})$: \begin{enumerate} \item On input $\mat{z} \in \mextension$, first run the list decoding algorithm for $\dual$, i.e. $RSListDecode_{(m, \gamma, (1-\alpha)m)}(\mat{z})$. This gives a polynomial size list of codewords in $\dual$. \item If there is a unique $\mat{x}$ in the list such that $\hw_{2^n}(\mat{z}-\mat{x}) \leq \left(\frac{1}{2}+\epsilon\right)m$, then output $\mat{x}$ else output $\mat{0}$. \end{enumerate} \end{mdframed} \item \label{step:9} Apply the unitary $(I \otimes \QFT_{2^n}^{-1})$ to the resulting state, i.e., compute \[(I \otimes \QFT_{2^n}^{-1})\left(U_{\mathsf{decode}} U_{\mathsf{add}}\right) \left( \ket{\hat{\Psi}} \otimes \ket{\hat{\Phi}} \right).\] \item Finally, measure the second register, and output the outcome $\mat{y}^* \in \bF_2^{mn}$. \end{enumerate}

elements.subsection-2411-015: Restriction to a constant number of solutions
elements.sent-2411-393: We now turn our attention to analyzing Gröbner basis methods, noting that they are generally applied to overdetermined systems.
elements.sent-2411-394: This section details how we can obtain such a system from our equations.
elements.sent-2411-395: While we can certainly apply generic techniques, we may not have sufficient control over the resulting overdetermined output.
elements.sent-2411-396: Therefore, we also present specific approaches whose output remains akin to $\mathcal{Q}$.
elements.sent-2411-397: Several algorithms have been proposed to transform an underdetermined system into an overdetermined one \cite{thomae2021, furue_pqc, hashimoto2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-398: The simplest of these, due to Thomae and Wolf \cite{thomae2021 , transforms an underdetermined MQ system with $M$ equations in $N>M$ variables into another MQ system with $M - \left\lfloor N/M \right\rfloor + 1$ equations and the same number of variables.}
elements.sent-2411-399: These algorithms were motivated by the cryptanalysis of multivariate schemes, where the system of equations given by the public key can be underdetermined.
elements.sent-2411-400: A typical example is the Unbalanced-Oil-and-Vinegar (UOV) \cite{EC:KipPatGou99 system.}
elements.sent-2411-401: Although the UOV system is underdetermined, it is significantly less so than the system $\mathcal{Q}$, as the ratio of the number of equations to the number of variables typically remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-402: Another important point is that the UOV public system is widely believed to behave like a random system\footnote{Concretely, a system on which we would apply the results known for Boolean semi-regular systems \cite[Definition 3.2.4]{bardet_these .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-403: In particular, to the best of our knowledge, the algorithms of \cite{thomae2021,furue_pqc,hashimoto2023 have not yet been analyzed on more structured equations.}
elements.sent-2411-404: Therefore, we do not know how they would perform on $\mathcal{Q}$.
elements.sent-2411-405: If the Thomae-Wolf algorithm works as expected, it will yield a quadratic system where the number of equations and variables is equal to \begin{equation}m - \lfloor \alpha m n / m \rfloor + 1 = m - \lfloor \alpha n \rfloor + 1 = n^2 - \lfloor \alpha n \rfloor + 1.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-096: \begin{equation}m - \lfloor \alpha m n / m \rfloor + 1 = m - \lfloor \alpha n \rfloor + 1 = n^2 - \lfloor \alpha n \rfloor + 1.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-406: On the one hand, it is evident that solving the same number of generic Boolean MQ equations in the same number of variables using state-of-the-art methods \cite{bettale2009,boolean has exponential complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-407: On the other hand, even though the Thomae-Wolf algorithm may disrupt much of the initial structure of $\mathcal{Q}$, we cannot assume that the output is generic.
elements.sent-2411-408: A more reasonable assumption is that this output system is easier to solve than a generic one but that the corresponding cost is unlikely to drop from exponential to polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-409: In our context, a straightforward way to restrict the number of solutions is to search for vectors satisfying $\mathcal{P}$ and which belong to a subcode $C' \subseteq C$ of dimension $\ell \leq k$ over $\ff{2^n}$.

elements.sent-2411-411: In particular, we do not necessarily need to target a subcode with specific properties.
elements.sent-2411-412: We obtain a similar problem with $k$ replaced by $\ell$, which has approximately $2^{\ell n - m}$ solutions.
elements.sent-2411-413: We may choose $\ell = m/n = n$ to make the number of solutions constant.
